title: The Market for Conflicted Advice
abstract: We present a model of the market for advice in which advisers have conflicts of interest and compete for heterogeneous customers through information provision. The competitive equilibrium features information dispersion and partial disclosure. Although conflicted fees lead to distorted information, they are irrelevant for customers' welfare: banning conflicted fees improves only the information quality, not customers' welfare. Instead, financial literacy education for the least informed customers can improve all customers' welfare because of a spillover effect. Furthermore, customers who trade through advisers realize lower average returns, which rationalizes empirical findings.

article.introduction:
Providing advice is an importantfunction of intermediaries. The compensation structure of intermediaries, however, often leads to conflicts of interest. For example, broker-dealer firms are compensated by commissions and fund distribution fees, and realtors receive fees only if they close a deal. Financial advice has received considerable attention from academics and regulators alike. It is well documented that conflicted advice leads to lower investment returns, which imposes a substantial loss on households.1As a result, many countries have started regulating adviser fees in an effort to eliminate conflicted advice. However, how markets for advice function and their consequences for investors remain unknown. To design effective policy, it is necessary to understand why customers find it optimal to trade through advisers and how advisers respond to regulation.
In this paper, we develop an equilibrium model in which the quality of advice, customers' investment decisions, and their choice of advisers are determined jointly in a frictionless matching market. We then use this model to analyze how regulation may affect customers' welfare, taking into account how advisers adjust their quality of advice in equilibrium. To capture advisers' conflict of interest, we assume that advisers (which we take to be brokers, retail financial advisers, or realtors) are compensated only if they successfully convince their customers to invest. This setup reflects the fact that brokers are compensated by distribution fees, which are set by fund issuers, and do not charge a separate fee for providing advice.2, 3In our setting, advisers compete for customers by providing information. Taking these misaligned incentives as given, we aim to understand the extent to which advisers can generate valuable information for customers in this competitive setting.
To shed further light on how such conflicts of interest affect different agents in the economy, we account for both customer and adviser heterogeneity. Customers differ in the quality of their ex ante information, while advisers differ in the value of their expertise. More precisely, each adviser is perfectly informed about one particular type of asset and assets differ in their information sensitivity. Thus, an adviser with expertise in a more information-sensitive asset has, in effect, more valuable information.
In our setting, customers understand the misaligned incentives of advisers and optimally select their advisers based on the information provided in equilibrium. After choosing an adviser, the customer receives information, rationally updates his beliefs, and makes his investment decision. Since providing advice takes time, each adviser is subject to a capacity constraint. For simplicity, we assume that each adviser can match with at most one customer and vice versa. Advisers are scarce, so not all customers can receive advice. Thus, our setting can be understood as a matching model with two-sided heterogeneity, where information provision determines the gains for both sides. The key equilibrium objects are the distribution of information quality, the value of information to customers, the profits of advisers, and the matching patterns.
We derive a notion of information quality that arises endogenously from the adviser's optimal choice of what information to provide. We show that information is dispersed and distorted because of the conflicted fee structure. Intuitively, since brokers are compensated by fee-upon-investment, they have an incentive to provide biased advice to extract higher profits. However, to attract a particular customer, an adviser must provide sufficiently valuable information that the customer does not prefer to match with someone else. This competition disciplines advisers and forces them to provide at least partially informative advice. Specifically, the least informed customers are the easiest to deceive, and all advisers compete for those customers. Thus, the gain from trading through advisers, relative to trading on their own, is higher for the less informed customers. Nevertheless, they still receive worse information in equilibrium and earn lower investment returns, compared to more informed customers. Our results therefore suggest that investment returns per se do not represent the value of advice.
We use our model to establish important results about regulation. Many countries (e.g., Australia, Italy, the United Kingdom, and the Netherlands; see CEA (2015, p. 25)) have started to restrict adviser fees based on the idea that if fees are driving the conflict of interest, capping or eliminating them should improve the advice provided to customers. However, this argument fails to account for how advisers might change their information policy in equilibrium. We show that when regulators lower advisers' fees, advisers provide worse information. This is because the utility of a customer in a matching equilibrium is uniquely pinned down such that his matched adviser would not profit from attracting his next-best competitor. Since fees and information quality are substitutes from customers' perspective, whether customers are compensated by better information or lower fees is irrelevant. We thus establish that when advisers are on the short side of the market, customers' welfare is independent of the fee structure—that is, surprisingly, regulating adviser fees do not improve customers' welfare.4
So what can regulators do? Since the utility of a customer is pinned down, what matters is the underlying distribution of financial literacy, that is, the distribution of customers' informedness. Financial literacy education has a positive spillover effect. Specifically, when some customers in the economy become better informed, other customers also benefit, even though the latter's level of informedness does not change. Intuitively, when a customer becomes better informed, other potential customers become more attractive for advisers. These customers become relatively scarce and receive higher utility.
Because customers endogenously choose whether to trade through advisers, our model has implications for which type of customers trade through advisers and how this choice affects their returns (i.e., self-directed versus broker-client trades). Specifically, customers who trade through advisers have lower investment returns compared to those who trade on their own. However, this is driven entirely by a selection effect. In equilibrium, the most informed customers do not receive advice, but they realize higher returns because their own information is already very precise. Importantly, lower returns do not mean that advice destroys value. Only relatively uninformed customers receive advice. If these customers traded on their own, their returns would be even lower.
We can also compare advisers with conflicted fees to advisers who only charge up-front payments. The difference in returns between advisers who charge up-front fees and advisers who receive kickbacks is often interpreted as the cost of conflicted payment, as discussed in CEA (2015). Our theory establishes that this interpretation is misleading: while returns are higher through fee-only advisers, customer's utilities are the same under these two structures.
If customers forgo some investment opportunities (i.e., are less willing to take risks) without further information, then seeking advice helps customers identify investment opportunities, so that customers are more willing to take risks. However, since an adviser may oversell a product due to the conflict of interest, customers may invest even when the payoff is negative. Consistent with empirical findings (see Foerster et al. (2017)), the model thus predicts that broker clients are more willing to take risks but have a lower return than self-directed investors. This result is related to existing models based on trust, for example, Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2015), who consider an environment in which money managers can decrease customers' risk aversion (i.e., anxiety). Although we do not model trust, one can interpret the extent to which a customer trusts an adviser's recommendation as endogenously determined by the quality of information. Since this value is endogenous, our framework sheds light on how returns and welfare would change in response to policy changes or different fee structures.
Finally, we note that in our model, we assume that customers receive a private signal about the asset payoff. This signal is subject to false positives, that is, customers may receive a positive signal when the asset payoff is negative, but not vice versa. As we show in SectionIV.A, our results are robust to different specifications of customers' signals. In particular, less informed customers continue to match with advisers with more valuable knowledge. We also show that changing fees does not change customer welfare in a more general framework.
Methodologically, our work builds on the Bayesian persuasion approach (advanced by Kamenica and Gentzkow (2011))5and the literature on matching markets.6Compared to models that study competition within Bayesian persuasion frameworks, our model is distinct in two ways. First, our paper is the first to study information provision in a model of decentralized competition with heterogeneity. The question in Gentzkow and Kamenica (2016) and Au and Kawai (2019) concerns how the degree of competition affects information provision in a setting with multiple senders and one receiver.7In our framework, the market is frictionless but advisers are competing for heterogeneous customers and have a capacity constraint. The question we focus on is how the underlying distribution of financial literacy affects the equilibrium value of information and how this in turn affects different customers with different levels of sophistication. This question is of first-order importance for policy, since less sophisticated customers are generally perceived as being more at risk.
Second, building on matching models, in our framework competition is captured by agents' matching decisions, and within the match, information provision is exclusive.8This setup makes our framework very tractable. Despite allowing for a general message space, we provide a closed-form characterization of the optimal policy. Specifically, the value of information to a customer is uniquely pinned down, so that it is indeed optimal for him to stay within the match, taking into account the value provided by other advisers.9
Turning to the literature on financial advice more specifically, a series of papers by Inderst and Ottaviani (2009,2012b) study the optimal compensation for a direct marketing agent (i.e., an adviser) and show that a conflict of interest arises endogenously.10They consider a setting in which producers compete through commissions paid to an adviser and advisers have concerns for suitability with the customer's needs. Here, we take these misaligned interests as given and focus on how advisers compete for heterogeneous customers. Advisers do not care about the customer's well-being, but they generate valuable information because of competition. Stoughton, Wu, and Zechner (2011) study the use of kickbacks to advisers when customers differ in wealth levels. They find that kickbacks always decrease customer welfare, whereas we find that customer welfare is the same with and without kickbacks.11Different from these previous works, we allow customers to invest in all assets without contacting an adviser (i.e., intermediary), and more importantly, we solve for the quality of advice in equilibrium. This distinction allows us to provide empirical predictions on customers' returns between broker-client versus self-directed channels. The economics here is also very different, since the value provided by an adviser is purely informational (not about choosing the asset itself).12
Finally, our paper relates to the recent literature on persuasion and information disclosure in finance. Recent works include Bouvard, Chaigneau, and Motta (2015), Trigilia (2016), Malenko and Malenko (2019), Malenko and Tsoy (2019), and Orlov, Zryumov, and Skrzypacz (2018).
The paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, we present the model and in SectionII, we characterize the equilibrium. We discuss implications for regulation in SectionIIIand we discuss the robustness of our model in SectionIV. In SectionV, we discuss empirical implications of our model. We conclude in SectionVI. All proofs are in AppendixA. We provide details on an alternative two-stage setup in AppendixB. We consider additional extensions in the Internet Appendix.
article.I. Model:
Each customerbcan potentially invest in all assets and receives a private signal about the quality of asset,x(s,b)=1{s≥bλ}. By construction, if an asset is good(s≥λ), customers always receive a positive signal(x=1). However, there are false positives ifbλ≤s<λ: when the asset payoff is negative, customers still receive a positive signal. Thus, a customer with higherbis more informed, since his signal has a lower probability of false positives.
Moreover, since making mistakes is more costly for an asset with higherland information reduces the likelihood of false positives, an asset with higherlis more information sensitive: information reduces the likelihood of false positives, which is more valuable wheneverlis higher. Figure1illustrates the customer's payoff.
There is a unit mass of heterogeneous advisers on the other side of the market. Each adviser has expertise in one particular type of assetland is perfectly informed about the realized statesof this asset. Since this is the only dimension along which advisers differ, we uselto denote an adviser's type. The adviser types are observable. Since an asset with a higherlis more information sensitive, an adviser who has expertise in an asset with higherlhas in effect more valuable information.
The distribution of adviser types is given by measureG(l), which has differentiable densityg(l)and domainL. Throughout the paper, we assume that the measure of customers is larger than the measure of advisers, so that advisers are on the short side of the market.14
To capture the conflict of interest between advisers and customers, we assume that an adviser receives a positive payoffαc>0from a customer whenever the customer invests in the asset. In the context of financial advisers, this payoff represents a commission paid by customers or a kickback that is priced and passed on to customers.
We consider a decentralized market for advice in which different advisers compete for different customers through information provision. The two-sided heterogeneity in our model captures two important dimensions of the market for information: customers (the demand side) differ in their ex ante information and hence their needs for additional information, and advisers (the supply side) differ in the value of the information they can provide. Maintaining a relationship between an adviser and a customer requires time or other resources. Thus, there is a capacity constraint. For simplicity, we assume that each adviser can serve only one customer and that each customer can obtain advice from only one adviser.
Figure2depicts the time line of our setup. Att=0, the matching takes place. Specifically, the matching decision and information policy within the match are determined simultaneously, subject to pairwise stability. Intuitively, to match with a particular customer, the information provided by an adviser must be valuable enough that the customer will not be better off by choosing another adviser. Analogously, matching with a given customer must provide sufficient value to the adviser. Within a given match, we assume that each adviser can commit to an information policy before the state is realized. Formally, an information policy consists of a signalσ:[0,1]→Δ(M)that maps the state into a distribution over messagesm∈M.15
Att=1, after the match is made, the statesrealizes, and customers receive information and make their investment decisions. Specifically, each customer first observes his private signal and then the messagemsent by the adviserlwith whom he matches. He then uses Bayes rule to form a posterior belief about the state and decides whether to invest. If he decides to invest in assetl, he must buy the asset through the adviserlwith whom he matches. The adviser then receives his commissionαc.
For assets about which he does not receive advice, each customer makes his investment decision based on his own private signal. Note that customers can always make decisions on their own forallassets. However, customers are assumed to choose one asset about which to receive advice. Thus, each customer will choose the adviser that gives him the highest information gain. Our setting is designed so that choosing an adviser is about choosing the value of the information provided by the adviser, not about choosing which asset to invest in. Since each customer can invest on his own regardless of which adviser he chooses, he will choose the adviser who provides him the most valuable information, which is the difference between the expected value under policy σ and the expected value of self-directed trade. We letU∼(b,l,σ)denote the customer'sgainfrom receiving advice.
We use the following parameter restrictions throughout the paper.

Assumption.(A1)11−F(b̲λ)((1−F(λ))r−(F(λ)−F(bλ))l¯)>αc. (A2)F(λ)−F(b¯λ)1−F(λ)l̲>αc.

Assumption (A1) guarantees that the asset is sufficiently valuable that, conditional on receiving a positive private signal (x=1), all customers will invest through an adviser even when they do not receive any additional information from the adviser. Thus, if we had a monopolistic adviser, she would choose an information policy that extracts all value from customers and leaves them with their autarky value. This highlights how our results are driven by competition. We characterize the case in which Assumption (A1) does not hold in the Internet Appendix. Assumption (A2) guarantees that all advisers can provide positive value to all customers, so that all matches are potentially profitable.
Our equilibrium concept is pairwise stability: if any two agents agree to match, they cannot be better off by matching with others. To formally define the equilibrium, we need to introduce some notation:H(b,l)is the measure of advisers with type belowlthat match with customers with type belowbon the product{B∪∅}×{L∪∅}, andV∼(b,l,σ)is the utility of adviserlmatching with customerbfor a given information policy σ. We define the customer's gainU∼(b,l,σ)analogously.
Definition 1.An equilibrium consists of matching decisionsH(b,l), information policies within each pairσ∗(b,l), and payoff functions for advisersV(l)and customersU(b)that satisfy the following conditions:


Condition (i) means that adviserlprefers her equilibrium matchband equilibrium information policyσ∗(b,l)over any other. This is true wheneverV(l)exceeds any value the adviser could obtain by matching with a different customerb′under some information policyσ′, subject to customerb′also preferring to deviate. The analog holds for the customer in condition (ii). The last condition is a standard market-clearing condition. Our setup can be nested in the standard matching model with nontransferable utilities. Existence of equilibria in this class of economies is established in Kaneko (1982).
As we show in AppendixB, our equilibrium can also be sustained in a two-stage game where each adviser posts only a single information policy,σ(l), which is not contingent on the customer's type. Essentially, the adviser's payoff in equation3depends only on the information policy and not the customer's type, so that the adviser posts only a single information policy. Thanks to this feature, our equilibrium outcome holds even if (i) customer types are unobservable and/or (ii) advisers do not have the ability to commit to offer different quality advice to different types.
We now discuss how our modeling assumptions map to observed patterns in markets for advice, how they relate to existing literature, and how to extend our setting.
The fee structure we use is widespread in markets for advice. Financial advisers receive commissions and kickbacks (see, e.g., Bergstresser, Chalmers, and Tufano (2009), Christoffersen, Evans, and Musto (2013), Anagol, Cole, and Sarkar (2017)) whenever they convince a customer to invest. Importantly, this fee is not negotiated between customer and adviser, but is instead set by a fund issuer. This is the case with 12-1b fees, which are split between fund issuer and broker, or with sales commissions. Throughout most of the paper, we treat the fee as fixed from the viewpoints of the adviser and the customer. We can also directly incorporate kickbacks into our model by defining the adviser's fee asα=α0+αc. Here, α0is the kickback, which is not directly paid by the customer. All of our results carry through in this case. In the Internet Appendix, we rationalize the fixed fee structure as the optimal contract between fund issuers and advisers. Under optimal contracting, all of our results on matching and information provision remain unchanged.
We model the market for advice as frictionless matching. Although some firms provide information to a broad audience, for example, via investment newsletters, we focus on settings in which the adviser provides information only to particular customers. Providing advice takes time and the adviser can therefore contact only a limited number of customers. In a survey, Chater, Huck, and Inderst (2010) find that 80% of purchasers of investment products in Europe bought the product in a face-to-face setting with an adviser. We normalize this number to one for simplicity. If we allowed each adviser to match with a fixed mass of customers, our results would remain qualitatively unchanged. Similarly, customers can match with a limited number of advisers, which constrains the “amount” of advice they can receive. This is in line with empirical evidence. A survey of equity investors in the United States, Investment Company Institute (2005) shows that two-thirds of customers who receive advice rely on only a single adviser.
Advisers compete by providing information. This type of competition is standard in the literature on credence goods (see Darby and Karni (1973), Pitchik and Schotter (1987), Dulleck and Kerschbamer (2006)) and in other studies on advice and certification (e.g., Lerner and Tirole (2006), Inderst and Ottaviani (2009,2012a), Bolton, Freixas, and Shapiro (2012)). Within a match, we assume that advisers can commit to their information policy before the state is realized, which is standard in the Bayesian persuasion literature. In reality, one can interpret this as advisers using certain predetermined sales systems and/or following certain rules that govern interactions with customers. For example, a review by the Canadian government, Financial Consumer Agency of Canada (2018), finds that retail banks employ automated sales systems. “The bank employee' s computer screen may highlight [...] leads for that customer. These leads tend to be generated by algorithms, prompting the employee to offer a range of products. [...] Staff are generally required to offer products they see in the computer-generated leads.” In addition, “employees are provided with conversation cues and scripts, which are intended to ensure that the most important terms, fees, and conditions are disclosed to consumers.” Thus, banks commit to disclose certain aspects of their products. The design of these sales systems and rule sets essentially determines the quality of advice. These systems are in place before a given interaction takes place, which can be captured by our two-stage game setting, where predetermined rules are the posted menu.
We assume that each adviser knows the payoff of exactly one asset typel. This is meant to capture the fact that different advisers have expertise about different asset classes (or insurance products or types of real estate) with different payoff characteristics. Since advisers do not have expertise in all possible assets, customers must make some decisions on their own. Indeed, many customers who receive financial advice also maintain self-managed investments (see, e.g., Hoechle et al. (2017)), which is consistent with our assumption. We consider the case in which advisers know about an interval of assets in the Internet Appendix. There, we show that our main results carry through: there is negative assortative matching, and less informed customers receive “worse” information, as long as the interval is small.
The structure of asset payoffs captures the fact that different assets have different risks. Since customers are prone to false positives, only the lossldetermines the value of advice. Instead of assuming that assets differ in the lossl, we could have them differ in “volatility” υ, so that the payoff is either υ or−υ. As discussed in SectionIV.A, our results hold when assets have different returns and/or volatility.
Alternatively, we can assume that the adviser's information is a reduced-form representation of the fit between customer and asset (e.g., how the asset matches the customer's risk preference or investment horizon). In this formulation, the statesis asset and customer specific. All of our results carry through under this interpretation. Similarly, our results continue to hold if the adviser knows strictly more than the customer, that is, the adviser knowsland after matching with a customer also learns that customer's private signal.
Advisers may be dissuaded from providing bad advice by penalties imposed by regulators, lawsuits, or a loss of reputation and future business. We can extend our model to take ex post penalties into account in reduced form. If, after receiving advice, the customer realizes a loss, the adviser must pay an (expected) penalty ρ. As long asρ<α, all qualitative results remain unchanged. In the context of financial advice, Egan, Matvos, and Seru (2018) document that “advisor misconduct” is pervasive, which suggests that implicit reputational or labor market penalties are not sufficiently severe to dissuade it.
article.II. Characterization:
We now solve the model in two steps. First, we characterize the Pareto-optimal policy within any potential match and for any utility level (SectionII.A). We show that it is a threshold policy. Next, we solve for the matching decisions (SectionII.B) and equilibrium utilities (SectionII.C), using pairwise stability.
We now solve the adviser's problem in equation4, keeping the match(b,l)and the customer's utilityu¯fixed. This makes problem4a Bayesian persuasion problem. Specifically, we show that within any potential match, the adviser's optimal policy can be characterized by a cutoffŝ∈[bλ,λ], without loss of generality. The adviser recommends investing if and only ifs≥ŝ(Proposition1). We can interpretŝas information quality, since a higherŝmeans that customers are less likely to invest in an asset with a negative payoff.
Consider a potential match(b,l). Our information structure implies that whenever a customer receives a bad signal(x=0), he knows for certain that the asset's payoff is negative. In this case, investing is never optimal for any message received from an adviser. We can therefore limit attention to the case in which the customer receives a good signal (x=1) when describing the optimal information policy. Upon receiving the good signal, the customer learns thats≥bλ, and her belief about the state is given by the pdff(s)1−F(bλ)with domain[bλ,1]. We write μ1for the corresponding measure.
The adviser's optimal policy takes the form of a threshold.19
Proposition 1.Without loss of generality, the optimal policy can be characterized by a thresholdŝ∈[bλ,λ]such that the adviser recommends investing if and only ifs≥ŝ.

We prove this result in AppendixA. The key steps are as follows. The customer's decision is binary: he either invests or does not invest. The adviser uses a direct recommendation policy with two messages, “invest” and “do not invest.” In equilibrium, the customer always follows the adviser's advice. When the asset paysr(i.e.,s≥λ), the adviser always recommends investing; otherwise, the policy would be Pareto dominated. So, only the likelihood of investing when the asset pays−lmatters for the values of the customer and the adviser. We prove that any such likelihood can be induced by a threshold policy, which recommends investing whenevers≥ŝ.
The threshold represents the quality of information. The higher the thresholdŝ, the better the information is, since the customer is less likely to have a false positive and invest if the asset's payoff is negative. Accordingly, we only consider threshold policies in the following.
We now analyze the matching patterns. First, as we can observe from problem4, conditional on providingu¯to a customer, an adviser would prefer to match with a less informed customer, that is,v(b,l,u¯)is decreasing inb. Intuitively, for any given information policy, the gain for a less informed customer is higher because he has worse information when he trades in the asset himself. The adviser can therefore use a lower threshold and obtain higher expected commissions while still deliveringu¯to the customer.21
Given that all advisers prefer to match with less informed customers, competition implies that less informed customers receive a higher utilityU(b)in equilibrium. Otherwise, every adviser would choose a lowerbto match with, which violates market clearing. Thus, the equilibrium gainU(b)must decrease inb.22
In equilibrium, an adviser takes customers' utilitiesU(b)as given and chooses a customer optimally, knowing that to attract a less informed customer, she must provide him a higher gain. The matching outcome is determined by which adviser is willing to provide a higher gain to attract the less informed customers. Specifically, the term(F(ŝ)−F(bλ))lin equation10implies that the value of information increases withlfor any givenŝbecause mistakes are more costly for an asset with a higherl. Hence, conditional on providing the same level of promised utility to a customer, an adviser with a higherlcan provide a lower thresholdŝ. In this sense, it is less costly for an adviser with a higherlto promise a higher utility to attract a less informed customer.
As a result, when two advisers compete to match with a less informed customer, the one with the higherlcan outbid his competitor, which leads to the following result.
Lemma 1. (Assortative Matching)A less informed customer matches with an adviser with more valuable expertise.

In the proof, we apply Corollary1in Legros and Newman (2007) to establish the sorting outcome. Furthermore, we show thatdv(b,l,U(b))dbdl<0, that is, if an adviser with a lowerlweakly prefers customerbover customerb′, whereb′>b, then an adviser with a higherl′must strictly prefer the less informed customerb. Given that the adviser's optimality condition can be rewritten asV(l)=maxbv(b,l,U(b)), the sorting outcome can then be alternatively understood as the comparative statics from the optimization problem: the type of customerbthat an adviserlchooses must be decreasing inl.
Because of Proposition1, the information policy can be summarized by a simple cutoff rule, which gives us a tractable expression for customers' utilities. Based on this expression, we now characterize the assignment functionℓ(b), the equilibrium payoffsU(b)andV(l), and the threshold used by each adviser. Lemma1implies that the assignment functionℓ(b)must be weakly decreasing.
Lets∗(l)denote the threshold policy that an adviserluses in equilibrium. Note that, conditional on a thresholdŝ, an adviser's payoff isαc(1−F(ŝ)), which is independent of the customer's type. This means that an adviserlis willing to match with any customer under the threshold policys∗(l)because this information policy guarantees that the adviser receives her equilibrium utility. When evaluating deviations from the customer perspective, it is without loss of generality to focus ons∗(l), as doing so gives the highest possible deviating payoff for customers and makes advisers indifferent.
Proposition 2.The unique equilibrium is characterized by an assignment functionℓ(b), payoff functionsU(b)andV(l), and an information policys∗(l)such that:

In equilibrium, a less informed customer must match with an adviser with a more information-sensitive asset and receive less precise information but must gain more by participating in the market. At the same time, adviser with a more information-sensitive asset must earn a higher profit. The equilibrium information policy is illustrated in Figure3.
The policy that maximizes the pairwise surplus is full disclosure. Thus, the information policy is distorted in equilibrium, even though there is competition between advisers. This is because advisers are compensated by a fixed commission—they compete through information provision, and the only way they can extract higher profits is to give more biased advice. This is in contrast to standard price competition, where advisers compete through fees. Specifically, since advisers and customers value information differently, this feature leads to imperfectly transferable utilities among agents, and thus the policy within the pair does not maximize the pairwise surplus.
In our setting, dispersed information is driven by the fact that heterogeneous agents compete for the right to match with a more attractive type in the market. As a result, advisers with more valuable expertise must receive a higher payoff and give less precise information. However, an adviser with a higherlmust also provide enough value to his equilibrium customer that the customer will not go to the next-best adviser (i.e., adviser with typel−ε). Such competition thus pins down the information quality for each matching pair.
If advisers are homogeneous and on the long side of market, then the unique equilibrium would feature full disclosure. The given distribution oflin our model thus captures the idea that expertise is scarce. If we instead had a single monopolistic adviser who knows about every asset, that adviser would provide no information and all customers would be left with their outside value.
article.III. Implications for Regulation:
To help discipline financial advisers, many countries (e.g., the United Kingdom, Australia, and the Netherlands) have restricted the conflicted fees that advisers can receive. The intuition is simple: if conflicted fees distort advisers' incentives, then reducing them should improve the quality of advice. However, this view does not take into account how advisers adjust their information policies in equilibrium. In this section, we formally evaluate such a policy via comparative statics on the conflicted feeαc. We find that, surprisingly, regulating the fee does not improve customer welfare.
In our model, competition disciplines advisers to provide valuable advice: advisers provide enough value to their customers that the customers do not prefer to match with another adviser. Specifically, customers' utility is described by equation13, which depends only on the assignment functionℓ(b)and the boundary conditionU(b∗)=0. Given that Lemma1holds for any fee structureαc, the assignment function (i.e., the matching outcome) remains the same. This immediately implies that customers' utilityU(b)is the same under any fee structure. Restricting the adviser's fees thus has no effect on customers' utilities. Notice that the above argument holds as long as the assignment function remains the same, so this result is independent of the sorting outcome being positive or negative.
Proposition 3.For any conflicted feeαc>0that satisfies Assumption (A1), customers receive the same utility. Furthermore, this utility is identical to what they would obtain under competitive pricing (i.e., without conflicted fees).

Another way to see this is to consider customers' utilities in equation12. Customers are better off with either a lower fee or better information (i.e., a higher thresholdŝ), that is, from customers' perspective, fees and information are substitutes. Since what matters for competition is customers' utilities, when feesαcdecrease, advisers respond by providingworseinformation in equilibrium. Hence, any policy that changes the conflicted fee will affect information quality, not customers' utilities.
Corollary 1.Lets∗(l;αc)denote the equilibrium information under feeαc. The lower the fee, the worse the information: for anyαc′<αc,s∗(l;αc′)<s∗(l;αc).

The second part of Proposition3compares our result to a setting in which there are no conflicted fees and advisers charge a competitive up-front payment instead. As we explain in the introduction, this corresponds to a fiduciary duty standard. Under both scenarios, customers' utilities are the same. Thus, even regulation that eliminates conflicted fees completely will not improve welfare. With up-front fees only, there is no need for advisers to distort information, and thus customers will receive full disclosure. However, advisers will extract customers' values via the up-front fee, so customers are no better off.
The mechanics behind this result are as follows. As long as the fees can be adjusted optimally, utility is perfectly transferable and the information policy must maximize pairwise surplus (i.e., full disclosure). Moreover, regardless of whether the fee is contingent on customers' investment decisions, the effective payment will be uniquely pinned down. Thus, without loss of generality, we consider the setting in which the flat fees and the information policy are determined jointly. Specifically, suppose that an adviserlcharges fees, denoted byγ∗(l), in addition to the information policy. Given the feeγ∗(l)and the information policys∗(l), customers make their matching decisions to maximize their expected utility.
Finally, note that information distortion decreases the pairwise surplus,(F(s∗(l))−F(bλ))l. This implies that while information quality does not affect customers' utilities, it must increase the profits of advisers. It follows that, advisers bear the cost of information distortions under fixed fees, with their profit highest under the competitive benchmark (i.e., full disclosure).
Corollary 2.The information distortion decreases the profit of advisers, but not the profit of consumers. A higher feeαcleads to higher profits for all advisers.

We now use our model to evaluate the effects of financial literacy education. Unlike in the case of regulating fees, changing the informedness of even a small segment of customers can force advisers to provide better information to all customers. This spillover effect unambiguously improves welfare.
For our purposes, financial literacy education takes relatively uninformed customers and makes them more informed. This changes the distribution of customer typesQ(b). Formally, consider two distributionsQ1(b)andQ0(b): both have the same domainB, butQ1(b)first-order stochastically dominatesQ0(b), that is,Q1(b)≤Q0(b)∀b∈BandQ1(b∗)=Q0(b∗). Intuitively,Q1(b)has more customers who are relatively informed. We takeQ1to be the result of a financial literacy intervention starting withQ0.
When more customers are more informed, the market-clearing condition requires that any given customerbmatch with an adviser with a (weakly) higherl. That is,ℓ0(b)≤ℓ1(b), whereℓj(b)represents the assignment under distributionQj(b). We can show via a comparison theorem on customers' utilitiesU(b)that the solution to the differential equation13underQ1(b)must be weakly higher.
Proposition 4.Consider two distributions with the same range, whereQ1(b)≤Q0(b)andQ1(b∗)=Q0(b∗). Customers' utilities are weakly higher underQ1(b)regardless of the fee structure:U1(b)≥U0(b).

Proposition4thus predicts that any given customerb̂is better off whenothersbecome more informed. Intuitively, advisers compete for less informed customers, since they are the ones who value information most. Thus, forb̂, when all other customersb<b̂become more informed, this means that his competitors become less attractive to advisers. Customerb̂must then receive higher utility. This result shows how competitive forces shape customers' utilities in the market with two-sided heterogeneity. Specifically, in equilibrium, all agents are compensated such that it is optimal for them to match to their counterparty instead of others. Hence, the distribution of other customers and advisers, which represents the distribution of potential competitors, is the key determinant of agents' profits.
In an environment with a fixed contingent fee, the only way advisers can offer customers a higher payoff is through information policy. Hence, the intuition above implies that advisers must offer better information and earn lower rents, as formalized below.
Corollary 3.In an environment with a fixed conflicted fee, consider two distributions with the same range, whereQ1(b)≤Q0(b)andQ1(b∗)=Q0(b∗). Information qualitys∗(l)is weakly higher and the adviser's profitV(l)is weakly lower underQ1(b).

In a similar exercise, one can also change the shape of the distribution of adviser typesG(l). For example, suppose thatG1(l)first-order stochastically dominatesG0(l), that is, there are fewer advisers with valuable information underG2(l). The same intuition applies: an adviser with expertise in a higherlbecomes more scarce, and as a result she must earn a higher profit, meaning worse information for customers.
article.IV. Robustness:
The economics behind our sorting result is that customers with higher demand for information (i.e., the less informed customers) gain more when matched with advisers with more valuable information (i.e., those who know about riskier assets). We now show that this result holds in a more general setting for the customer's information structure and for different sources of heterogeneity.
In SectionI, the customer's information structure is modeled as false positives. This setting allows us to summarize “information quality” with a single threshold. Moreover, the Pareto frontier exhibits a tractable and differentiable form. We now consider a more general environment in which we do not explicitly specify the customer's information structure.
As before, letbindex the quality of a customer's information, so that more informed customers have a higher payoff under self-directed trade for all assets: forb′>b,u0(b′,l)>u0(b,l). We restrict our attention to settings in which the information policy σ is such that customers always invest when the asset payoff is positive.23LetqL(b,l,σ)denote the ex ante probability under policy σ that a customerbinvests in assetlwhen the payoff is negative. We can interpretqLas the likelihood that the customer invests in a bad asset.
Proposition 5.For any information policy σ under which customers always invest when the asset payoff is positive, less informed customers are matched with advisers with more valuable expertise, and receive worse information, as long as, for anyb′>bandl′>l,

By definition, a more informed investor receives a higher payoff without advice, which is captured by the difference in payoffu0(b′,l)−u0(b,l)>0. Proposition5establishes that the sorting result is robust to considering more general structures as long as the difference in the payoff is higher for more information-sensitive assets (condition16).
In our baseline model with false positives, this condition is captured by a less informed investor being more likely to make mistakes (i.e., overinvest) without an adviser and making mistakes being more costly for assets with higherl. If all customers choose not to invest in assetlwithout advice (i.e., without Assumption (A1)) and instead receive some default outside option that does not depend onl, then there is no sorting. We characterize this case in the Internet Appendix.
The above logic suggests that the exact source of the customer's mistakes is less relevant for our results. In the false positive case (our baseline model), customers invest “too much” based on their own signal. Alternatively, one can consider the false negative case, where customers invest “too little” based on their own signal. This is because under false negatives, their own signal may be low even though the asset payoff is positive. In this case, the less informed customers are the ones who are more likely to receive a negative signal and forgo investment opportunities under self-directed trade.
Formally, the signal structure for customers isx=1{s≥λb}, whereb∈[λ,1]. Since the information value now relates to the upside, we assume that all assets have the same downside risklbut differ in their upside returnr. Thus, an asset with a higher returnris more information sensitive. The outside option for customers isu0(b,r)=(1−F(λb))rand hence condition16continues to hold here. As before, less informed customers match with advisers who have more valuable expertise (i.e., the adviser with a higherrin this case).24
Under both information environments, we have assumed that assets differ in only one dimension, with the dimension of heterogeneity designed to capture the value of information, (i.e., downside risk (l) for the false positive case and upside return (r) for the false negative case). The sorting result holds, however, for more general asset payoffs. For example, for the false positive case, one can allow the upside return to be asset specific, denoted byrl. From equation2, this change does not affect the cross-partials of agents' outside optionubl0(b,l), which means that condition16must hold and thus the sorting outcome remains the same.
The economics behind the sorting is also robust to considering different notions of heterogeneity. For example, one can consider an alternative modeling of “better” advisers, where all advisers know about the same assetlbut differ in how informed they are. Specifically, let δ index advisers' types, where advisers with type δ learn about the stateswith probability δ but learn nothing with probability1−δand cannot give advice. Advisers are heterogeneous in δ. Customers' information structure and preferences remain the same as in our main model. We can show that the model can be nested as if the outside option becomesδu0(b,l). Thus, for anyδ′>δ, we haveδ′(u0(b′,l)−u0(b,l))>δ(u0(b′,l)−u0(b,l))and less informed customers are matched with more informed advisers.
On the customer side, another factor that could drive the demand for information is customer wealth. The intuition for our sorting result can be easily extended to this setting: wealthier customers (i.e., those with higher demand for information) are matched with advisers with more valuable expertise. This result is consistent with the empirical literature (see, e.g., Hackethal, Haliassos, and Jappelli (2012), Hoechle et al. (2017)), which shows that wealthier clients have access to more knowledgeable advisers.
Proposition 6.When customers differ in wealth, a wealthier customer gains more by participating in the market for advice, and he is matched with an adviser with more valuable expertise.

Although our settings with heterogeneous sophistication and heterogeneous wealth may differ on the surface, the key driver of the matching outcomes is the same: customers who value advice more are matched with advisers with more valuable expertise. Since these two dimensions may not be perfectly correlated in practice, our results suggest that it would be useful for empirical research to consider customers' sophistication in addition to wealth as determinants of getting advice.
We now show how our irrelevance result changes when advisers are on the long side of the market. The key economics behind our irrelevance result is that fees and information are substitutes. Specifically, taking into account both fees and information, equation13uniquely pins downs how much a customer should be compensated relative to her next-best competitor (i.e.,U′(b)). Since lower fees reduce the cost of receiving worse information, a less informed customer is now given a lower threshold so that hisrelativegain remains the same. This result is independent of who is on the short side of the market, that is, Corollary1remains intact.
article.V. Implications for Empirical Work:
Prior empirical evidence documents that conflicted advice leads to lower investment returns (e.g., Bergstresser, Chalmers, and Tufano (2009), Chalmers and Reuter (2010), Hoechle et al. (2017)), which raises the question of why customers seek advice in the first place. The same phenomenon occurs in our model, but we show that customers rationally choose to receive advice. This result is driven by two factors. First, in the context of our model, observed investment returns do not fully reflect a customer's value from participating in the market for advice, as they do not take the customer's outside option into account. Intuitively, since less informed customers make worse investment decisions on their own, they prefer to participate in the market even when they receive worse advice and realize lower returns. Second, there is a selection effect. Customers who receive no advice have higher investment returns than those who do, but this is because only the most informed customers choose to forgo advice in equilibrium.25
In the case of false negatives in SectionIV.A, this result is even more stark. There, if a customer invests without receiving any advice, his return is alwaysr. When he receives advice, the adviser sometimes recommends that he invest in an asset with a negative return, so conditional on receiving advice, the return is always lower. Advice still improves value for customers, however, because without advice they would reject too many investments that have positive returns. This again shows that it is optimal for customers to seek conflicted advice, even though doing so leads to a lower return.
Another common way to measure the cost of conflicted advice is to compare the return across fee-only and commission-based advisers. The difference in these returns is often interpreted as the cost of conflicted fees, as discussed in CEA (2015). In our model, customers of fee-only advisers can realize higher investment returns, but their utility from receiving advice is the same.
Specifically, with fee-only advisers, customers always receive full disclosure. Thus, the return after the fee is simplyrminus the competitive flat feeγ(l),RNC(l)=r−γ(l). One can see that the return is higher than that under conflicted fees, that is,RNC(l)>R(l). However, as we show in Proposition3, customer utility will be the same across the two settings. Customers simply pay in different ways.
article.VI. Conclusion:
In this paper, we study a matching model for information in which advisers are subject to conflicts of interest. In contrast to existing models that assume naive customers, our model assumes rational customers with different levels of sophistication and analyzes the quality of information under competition between advisers. We are thus able to establish new insights and predictions regarding customers' welfare and asset returns. Specifically, we show that it is the underlying distribution of financial literacy that determines customers' welfare—when advisers are scarce, advisers' fee structure is irrelevant for customers' welfare. We further show that existing empirical measures of conflicted advice can be misleading if they fail to account for the selection effect whereby only the most informed customers choose to forgo advice in equilibrium.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Proof of Proposition1:
To prove the proposition, we must establish a few preliminary results. The following lemma shows that the optimal policy induces at most two posterior beliefs.
Lemma A1.For any information policyPsuch thatU∼(b,l,P)>u0(b,l), there exists an equivalent policyP̂that places weight



Proof.The result follows from the law of iterated expectations. Consider an information policyP. The values of the adviser and customer are given by


We next show that, without loss of generality, any optimal policy is a threshold policy. That is, the adviser truthfully reveals whethersis above some thresholdŝ. We do so by showing that threshold policies span the Pareto frontier within the match between customer and adviser. We call any policy that achieves the Pareto frontierefficient.

This expression implies thatvbl<0andvlu¯>0. According to Corollary1in Legros and Newman (2007), this implies in turn that less informed customers are matched with advisers with higherl, that is, we have negative assortative matching.
Equivalently, this means thatdv(b,l,U(b))dbdl=vbl(b,l,U(b))+vul(b,l,U(b))U′(b)<0, becauseU′(b)<0. Since an adviser's choice of customers in equilibrium can be expressed asmaxbv(b,l,U(b)), one can understand the sorting outcome by relying on the standard argument from monotone comparative statics, which suggests that an adviser with a higherlmust choose a lowerb.
article.Proof of Proposition2: We can apply the Picard-Lindelöf theorem to show that the differential equations characterizing the assignment function and customer utility (equations11and13, respectively) have unique solutions given the initial conditions forU(b∗)andℓ(b∗). As we argue in SectionII.B, in any equilibrium,ℓ(b)andU(b)must satisfy these differential equations. Therefore, the equilibrium is unique.
article.Proof of Proposition4: When more customers are more informed, the market-clearing condition requires that any given customerbmust match with an adviser with a (weakly) higherl. Formally, letℓj(b)represent the assignment under distributionQj(b). Then, the market-clearing condition requires∫b̲bdQj(b∼)=∫ℓj(b)l¯dG(l)∼. Hence, ifQ1(b)≤Q0(b), we haveℓ0(b)≤ℓ1(b). Recall that customers' utilitiesUj(b)are the solution to differential equation13, which impliesU1′(b)<U0′(b)<0. SinceUj(b∗)=0, by a comparison theorem we haveU1(b)≥U0(b)∀b≤b∗.
article.Proof of Proposition5: To establish the sorting for a more general environment, we employ the condition identified in Proposition1of Legros and Newman (2007), which does not require differentiability. The idea behind the proof is to show that if a more informed customerb′is indifferent betweenlandl′, then a less informed customerbmust strictly prefer to match with an adviser with a higherl. Formally, this means that ifu(b′,l′,v(b′,l′,u¯))=u(b′,l,v(b′,l,u¯)), thenu(b,l′,v(b′,l′,u¯))>u(b,l,v(b′,l,u¯)).
article.Appendix B: Alternative Setting with Two:
This section provides an alternative interpretation of our setup with the following extensive form. First, each adviser posts a menu of information policies contingent on customers' typesbto maximize her expected payoff. That is, each adviserlposts a menuσm(b,l). Second, customers choose their adviser given the posted information policy. Then, as before, the state realizes and customers make their investment decisions based on the information they receive.
One can use the equilibrium concept from the literature on large games (e.g., Mas-Colell (1984)), where the payoff of each agent is determined by his own decision as well as by the distribution of others' actions in the market. Given the distribution of other agents' actions in the market, one can then define equilibrium utility as the highest utility the agent can obtain. The adviser then chooses the information policy and the customer type she wants to attract, taking the equilibrium utility as given.
To see that posting the menuσm(b,l)is indeed an equilibrium in this setting, one needs to check two conditions. First, given the posted informationσm(b,l), a customerbwill not benefit from choosing a different adviserl′that is not on the support ofH(b,l). That is,U(b)=U∼(b,l,σm(b,l))≥U∼(b,l′,σm(b,l′))∀l. The main difference from our main setup is that, when a customer chooses a different adviserl′, the information policy that he obtains in this two-stage game isσm(b,l′); our original equilibrium allows for any deviating policy as long as it attracts adviserl′. Our equilibrium condition (ii) guarantees that there is no policy that can make a customer better off while making the adviser also (weakly) better off. The constructed menuσm(b,l′)in equationB1guarantees that an adviserl′receives her equilibrium utility when matching with any customerb. Hence,choosingσm(b,l′)cannot be profitable for customerbin this two-stage setting; otherwise, matching with adviserl′with information policyσm(b,l′)constitutes a profitable deviation in our matching game, which would violate pairwise stability.
Second, advisers will not choose a different combination of customer and information policy. The argument above guarantees that, given the menuσm(b,l), customerbonly chooses the adviserlthat is on the support ofH(b,l). Hence, if an adviser wants to successfully attract a different customerb′, the policy that she provides must ensure that the customer receives at leastU(b′). Her maximum payoff under this deviation is given bymaxσ{V∼(b′,l,σ)|U∼(b′,l,σ)≥U(b′)}≤V(l), where the last inequality follows from our equilibrium condition (i).
The argument above leads to the following result.
Proposition B1.The equilibrium outcome{U(b),V(l),H(b,l),σ∗(b,l)}in our matching game in SectionIIremains an equilibrium in the game in which each adviser posts a menuσm(b,l)and each customer chooses which adviser to match with.

Two additional observations are in order. First, the solutionσm(b,l)to equationB1is the thresholdσm(b,l)that gives adviserlhis equilibrium payoffV(l). Conditional on the thresholdŝ, advisers do not care about a customer's type, which implies that the threshold strategy that solvesV∼(b,l,ŝ)=αcw(1−F(ŝ))=V(l)is the same for all customers, that is, all advisers post a single policys∗(l)for all customersbin this two-stage game. This result implies that our equilibrium remains the same in a setting in which advisers are restricted to use a noncontingent policy.
Moreover, one can guess and verify that the equilibrium outcome{U(b),V(l),s∗(l)}in our matching model in SectionIremains an equilibrium in a setting where customers' types are not observable. To see this, note that, given the posted policys∗(l), the optimization problem remains the same from the perspective of customers, which is described by equation9. Thus, customers have no incentive to deviate, so their utilityU(b)is the same.
It remains to show that an adviser will not post a different policy. When posting a different policy, an adviser now has a rational belief about which type to attract. Nevertheless, he can only attract a customer type conditional on that type giving him his equilibrium utilityU(b). However, we have argued that an adviser will not benefit from attracting a different customer under the constructed equilibrium. Thus, for the same reason, there is no profitable deviation for advisers.
Notice that both features above are driven by the fact that an adviser does not care about a customer's type, conditional on the threshold. This result is not true, however, in a more general setting. For example, when buyers differ in wealth, then conditional on the threshold, an adviser is better off when matching with a wealthier customer. Specifically,V∼(w,l,ŝ)=αcw(1−F(ŝ)), and hence the thresholdσm(w,l)that gives the adviserlhis equilibrium payoffV(l)is no longer the same across all buyers, which is now given byσm(w,l)=s−1(V(l)w). That is, a wealthier customer now receives a better threshold. This also implies that the same outcome can no longer be sustained under private information, as buyers with lower wealth have incentives to pretend that they have higher wealth.
